subject: Algebra

title: Algebra: Quadratic Equations — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of quadratic equations concepts in Algebra.
subject: Algebra
keyPoints: A quadratic equation has the form ax² + bx + c = 0, where a ≠ 0. The graph of a quadratic function y = ax² + bx + c is a parabola.
Standard form: ax² + bx + c = 0. Vertex form: a(x - h)² + k = 0, where (h, k) is the vertex.
The vertex is the highest or lowest point on the parabola. If a > 0, the parabola opens up (minimum). If a < 0, it opens down (maximum).

subject: Algebra

The y-intercept is always (0, c). The x-intercepts are the solutions to ax² + bx + c = 0.
For a quick sketch: vertex, y-intercept, and one point on each side of the axis.

title: Biology: Cell Biology — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of cell biology concepts in Biology.
subject: Biology
keyPoints: The cell is the basic unit of life. All living organisms are made of cells — some have just one (unicellular), others have trillions (multicellular).
Prokaryotic cells: Simple, no nucleus. Bacteria are prokaryotes. DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm.
Eukaryotic cells: Complex, have a nucleus that holds DNA. Plants, animals, fungi, and protists are eukaryotes.
Key organelles in animal cells:

title: Biology: Cell Biology — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of cell biology concepts in Biology.
subject: Biology
keyPoints: Meiosis produces sex cells (gametes) with half the chromosome number. It involves two rounds of division: Meiosis I and Meiosis II.
Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes. Meiosis II separates sister chromatids.

description: A visual exploration of genetics concepts in Biology.
subject: Biology

Law of Segregation: Each organism has two alleles for each trait, which separate during gamete formation
Law of Independent Assortment: Genes for different traits separate independently

description: A visual exploration of genetics concepts in Biology.
subject: Biology
keyPoints: Punnett squares predict the probability of offspring inheriting specific traits. A monohybrid cross tracks one trait, a dihybrid cross tracks two.
Dominant alleles (capital letter) mask recessive alleles (lowercase).
Example: T = tall (dominant), t = short (recessive).
Tt × Tt: Genotype ratio 1:2:1 (TT, Tt, tt). Phenotype ratio 3:1 (tall:short).

Provides ecosystem services (pollination, water purification)
Supports food security

title: World History: The Renaissance — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of the renaissance concepts in World History.
subject: World History
keyPoints: The Scientific Revolution began during the Renaissance as thinkers challenged ancient authorities and emphasized observation and experimentation.
Nicolaus Copernicus proposed that the Sun (not Earth) is the center of the solar system — a radical idea that challenged Church teaching.
Galileo Galilei improved the telescope and confirmed Copernicus's model. He was tried by the Church and forced to recant.
Andreas Vesalius revolutionized anatomy by dissecting human cadavers.

title: English: Grammar — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of grammar concepts in English.
subject: English
keyPoints: Every word in English belongs to one of eight categories called parts of speech:
Noun: A person, place, thing, or idea (dog, freedom, Paris)
Pronoun: Replaces a noun (she, they, it)
Verb: Action or state of being (run, is, believe)

title: English: Grammar — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of grammar concepts in English.
subject: English
keyPoints: Pronouns must agree with their antecedent (the word they replace) in number and gender.
Correct: 'Each student should bring his or her book.' or 'All students should bring their books.'
Incorrect: 'Each student should bring their book.' (traditional grammar considers this incorrect, though it's now commonly accepted)
Common pronoun errors:

title: English: Writing — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of writing concepts in English.
subject: English
keyPoints: The five-paragraph essay is the standard structure for academic writing:
Introduction: Hook, background, thesis statement
Body paragraph 1: First main point with evidence and analysis
Body paragraph 2: Second main point with evidence and analysis

title: English: Writing — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of writing concepts in English.
subject: English
keyPoints: The writing process has five stages:
Prewriting: Brainstorm ideas, research, create an outline. Don't worry about organization yet — just get ideas down.
Drafting: Write a rough version. Focus on getting your ideas on paper, not perfection. Follow your outline.
Revising: Improve content, organization, and clarity. Add details, cut irrelevant parts, reorganize paragraphs. This is the most important stage.

title: English: Literature — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of literature concepts in English.
subject: English
keyPoints: Literary analysis examines how authors use techniques to create meaning. When analyzing a text, consider:
Plot: The sequence of events. Exposition → rising action → climax → falling action → resolution.
Character: Protagonist (main character), antagonist (opposing force), round (complex), flat (simple).
Setting: Time and place, which influences mood and theme.

Stream of consciousness: Writing that mimics the flow of thoughts
Fragmented narrative: Non-linear timelines
Focus on inner experience over external plot

description: A visual exploration of periodic table concepts in Chemistry.
subject: Chemistry
keyPoints: Rows are called periods (7 total). Elements in the same period have the same number of electron shells.
Columns are called groups (18 total). Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons and similar chemical properties.

subject: Chemistry
keyPoints: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons. They have the same atomic number (protons) but different mass numbers.
Carbon-12 (6 protons + 6 neutrons) — stable
Carbon-13 (6 + 7) — stable
Carbon-14 (6 + 8) — radioactive, used for carbon dating

keyPoints: Chemical reactions involve rearranging atoms to form new substances. Reactants → Products.
Evidence of a reaction:
Temperature change (exothermic = heat released, endothermic = heat absorbed)
Gas production (bubbles)

title: Chemistry: Chemical Reactions — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of chemical reactions concepts in Chemistry.
subject: Chemistry
keyPoints: Acids and bases are opposites in chemistry:
Acids: Taste sour, pH < 7, turn blue litmus red. Examples: HCl (hydrochloric), H₂SO₄ (sulfuric), CH₃COOH (acetic).
Bases: Taste bitter, feel slippery, pH > 7, turn red litmus blue. Examples: NaOH (sodium hydroxide), NH₃ (ammonia).
pH scale: 0 (strongly acidic) to 14 (strongly basic). 7 is neutral (pure water).

title: Chemistry: Atomic Structure — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of atomic structure concepts in Chemistry.
subject: Chemistry
keyPoints: Atoms are the basic units of matter. They consist of a nucleus (protons + neutrons) surrounded by electrons.
Protons: Positive charge, mass ~1 amu, found in nucleus. Number = atomic number = element identity.
Neutrons: No charge, mass ~1 amu, found in nucleus. Number can vary (isotopes).
Electrons: Negative charge, negligible mass, found in orbitals around nucleus. Determine chemical properties.

title: Chemistry: Atomic Structure — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of atomic structure concepts in Chemistry.
subject: Chemistry
keyPoints: Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractions BETWEEN molecules (not inside them):
London dispersion forces: Present in all molecules. Temporary dipoles. Strength increases with molecular size.
Dipole-dipole forces: Between polar molecules. The positive end of one molecule attracts the negative end of another.
Hydrogen bonding: Strong dipole-dipole force when H is bonded to N, O, or F. Responsible for water's unique properties.

title: Geometry: Triangles — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of triangles concepts in Geometry.
subject: Geometry
keyPoints: Similar triangles have the same shape but different sizes. Corresponding angles are equal, and corresponding sides are proportional.
The ratio of corresponding sides is called the scale factor.
AA (Angle-Angle) Similarity: If two angles are equal, the triangles are similar.
SSS Similarity: If all three side ratios are equal, the triangles are similar.

title: Geometry: Circles — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of circles concepts in Geometry.
subject: Geometry
keyPoints: A circle is the set of all points at a fixed distance (radius) from a center point. Key terms:
Radius (r): Distance from center to any point on the circle
Diameter (d): Distance across the circle through center. d = 2r
Circumference (C): Distance around the circle. C = 2πr = πd

title: Geometry: Circles — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of circles concepts in Geometry.
subject: Geometry
keyPoints: Area of a sector = (central angle / 360°) × πr².
Area of a segment = Area of sector - Area of triangle.
Example: A 90° sector in a circle of radius 4. Area = (90/360) × π(16) = (1/4) × 16π = 4π.
Applications: calculating pizza slice areas, designing wheels and gears, analyzing planetary orbits.

subject: Geometry
keyPoints: The equation of a straight line can be derived from:
Two points: Find slope m = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁). Then use point-slope form.
Point and slope: y - y₁ = m(x - x₁).
Graphical method: Read the y-intercept and slope from the graph.

Example: A 1000 kg car moving at 20 m/s has KE = ½(1000)(400) = 200,000 J

title: Physics: Energy — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of energy concepts in Physics.
subject: Physics
keyPoints: The law of conservation of energy in real systems:
Efficiency = (useful output energy / total input energy) × 100%.
Example: A light bulb converts 10% of electrical energy to light, 90% to heat. Efficiency = 10%.

title: Physics: Waves & Sound — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of waves & sound concepts in Physics.
subject: Physics
keyPoints: Waves transfer energy without transferring matter. They are disturbances that travel through a medium (or empty space for light).
Transverse waves: Particles move perpendicular to wave direction. Example: light, waves on a string, water waves.
Longitudinal waves: Particles move parallel to wave direction. Example: sound waves, compression waves in a spring.
Wave properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), speed (v).

title: Physics: Waves & Sound — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of waves & sound concepts in Physics.
subject: Physics
keyPoints: Applications of wave physics:
Ultrasound: High-frequency sound waves used for medical imaging. Sound reflects off tissue boundaries to create images.
Seismic waves: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth. Used to study Earth's interior and detect earthquakes.
Fiber optics: Light travels through glass fibers by total internal reflection. Used for high-speed internet.

title: Psychology: Learning & Memory — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of learning & memory concepts in Psychology.
subject: Psychology
keyPoints: Structuralism: Analyzed consciousness into basic elements (Wundt, Titchener)
Functionalism: Studied how mental processes help us adapt (William James)
Behaviorism: Focused only on observable behavior (Watson, Skinner)

title: Psychology: Learning & Memory — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of learning & memory concepts in Psychology.
subject: Psychology
keyPoints: Improving memory: Evidence-based strategies.
Spaced repetition: Review material at increasing intervals over time (much more effective than cramming).
Active recall: Test yourself instead of re-reading. Force your brain to retrieve information.
Elaboration: Connect new information to what you already know. Explain concepts in your own words.

title: Psychology: Brain & Behavior — Video 1
description: A visual exploration of brain & behavior concepts in Psychology.
subject: Psychology
keyPoints: Neurons are the basic building blocks of the nervous system:
Structure: Dendrites (receive signals) → cell body → axon (transmits signal) → axon terminals (send signals to next neuron).
Synapse: The gap between neurons. Neurotransmitters are released, cross the synapse, and bind to receptors on the next neuron.

title: Psychology: Brain & Behavior — Video 2
description: A visual exploration of brain & behavior concepts in Psychology.
subject: Psychology
keyPoints: Emotion and the brain:
The James-Lange theory: We feel emotion BECAUSE of physiological responses. We see a bear, our heart races, we feel fear.
Cannon-Bard theory: Physiological response and emotional experience occur simultaneously, independently.
